metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly label §3 as out-of-scope for this iteration) that covers the email-fallback /user/search bootstrap path: e.g. 'when an identity has email but no jira mapping, validate_assignee_exists calls /user/search?query=<email> and returns the resolved accountId; when /user/search returns empty, it degrades to the existing path (assert via stub call-count and return value)'.
findings.finding: Section 3 (transient /user/search bootstrap) is described under 'What' as a piece of implemented behavior but has NO corresponding acceptance criterion. None of the four ACs test the path where an identity has an email but no {provider:'jira'} mapping and the /user/search fallback resolves an accountId. A step with no verifying criterion is unverifiable as done.
findings.location: What §3 '/user/search bootstrap'; Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC5: 'Given a fixture identity with email but no {provider:"jira"} mapping, validate_assignee_exists calls /user/search?query=<email> (stub asserts call-count ≥ 1) and returns the resolved accountId; when the stub returns empty, validate_assignee_exists falls back to the existing assignable-search path (call-count on fallback ≥ 1).' If the bootstrap is intentionally untested this iteration, move §3 to 'Out of scope' and remove it from file_impact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section 4 that `reporter` must be POPPED from `fields` before `_update_one_filter_fields` (mirroring `_update_one_apply_parent`'s pop of `parent`) so it is never forwarded to the scalar `client.update_issue(**fields)` ACLI call — adding it to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` alone is insufficient and contradicts the 'NOT into the scalar update_issue' requirement.
findings.finding: The plan says to add `reporter` to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` in `dispatch_one.py` but ALSO says `update_one` routes reporter to `set_reporter` (NOT into the scalar `update_issue` call). With the current `_update_one_filter_fields` / `_update_one_scalar_update` architecture, any field in `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` flows directly into `client.update_issue(**fields)` — only `parent` is specially popped before that filter. Adding `reporter` to the allowlist without also popping it before `_update_one_filter_fields` (as `parent` is popped in `_update_one_apply_parent`) will send `reporter` into the ACLI subprocess call, contradicting the plan's explicit 'NOT into the scalar update_issue' requirement.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId) / dispatch_one.py _OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST + update_one routing
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reporter is added to dispatch_one.py::_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST' with: 'reporter is intercepted by a new _update_one_apply_reporter phase in update_one (analogous to _update_one_apply_parent) that pops reporter from fields before _update_one_filter_fields and routes it to set_reporter; reporter is NOT added to _OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in §4 that `reporter` must be popped from `fields` in a new `_update_one_apply_reporter` phase (mirroring `_update_one_apply_parent`) BEFORE `_update_one_filter_fields` runs, and that it should NOT be added to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` (which feeds the scalar ACLI call); the dedicated `set_reporter` REST sub-call is the only application path.
findings.finding: The plan adds `reporter` to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` and routes it to `set_reporter` via `update_one`, but does not specify that `reporter` must be POPPED from the fields dict BEFORE `_update_one_filter_fields` applies the allowlist — exactly as `parent` is popped via `_update_one_apply_parent`. Without an explicit pop+route phase, `reporter` will pass through the allowlist filter and be forwarded to `client.update_issue(**fields)`, which hands it to the ACLI scalar call that does not support reporter, silently no-oping or raising.
findings.location: ## What / §4 'Reporter by accountId — a DEDICATED REST sub-call'; ## file_impact dispatch_one.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the pop-before-filter protocol for `reporter` in `update_one` (analogous to `_update_one_apply_parent` popping `parent`), so that `reporter` is extracted and routed to `set_reporter` before `_update_one_filter_fields` runs — preventing double-application via the scalar `client.update_issue` call.
findings.finding: `reporter` is specified to be added to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` AND routed to a dedicated `set_reporter` sub-call — but the plan never specifies the pop-before-allowlist protocol inside `update_one` that prevents reporter from also being passed to `client.update_issue` in the scalar call. The existing `parent` field uses exactly this pattern (`_update_one_apply_parent` pops `parent` from `fields` before `_update_one_filter_fields`), but no analogous step is described for `reporter`.
findings.location: Section 4 (reporter by accountId) / file_impact: dispatch_one.py (_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST; update_one routes reporter to set_reporter sub-call)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_update_one_apply_reporter(fields, issue_key, client, ctx)` phase (or inline equivalent) that pops `reporter` from `fields`, resolves the accountId via `identity.jira_account_id`, calls `set_reporter`, catches `urllib.error.HTTPError` 4xx, and records the alert — mirroring the `_update_one_apply_parent` pattern. Remove `reporter` from `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` since it is never meant to reach the scalar ACLI call.

findings.finding: The plan states (Section 4) that `reporter` must be added to the 'outbound field-mapping layer (`outbound_fields.py`)'. However, `_map_local_to_jira_fields` in `outbound_fields.py` maps fields that already exist on the local ticket dict (title→summary, description, status, assignee, etc.). Local tickets have no `reporter` field in their schema — the plan does not identify what local-ticket field maps to `reporter`, nor how the identity-resolved accountId is injected into the mapping layer rather than resolved at apply time.
findings.location: Section 4 — Reporter by accountId, `outbound_fields.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) that explicitly covers the Section 3 bootstrap path: 'when an identity has an email but no jira mapping, the transient /user/search stub returns an accountId that validate_assignee_exists uses, and when /user/search returns empty the handler degrades gracefully without raising'.
findings.finding: Section 3 describes a transient `/user/search` bootstrap path (email → accountId for current run only) as a deliverable with specific behavior (best-effort, degrades on hidden email, no write-back), but no AC verifies this path's behavior — neither the success case (email found → accountId returned) nor the degradation case (hidden email → falls through gracefully).
findings.location: Section 3 (transient /user/search bootstrap) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC4)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test assertion (or extend AC4) that a change to the reporter field causes the differ to emit a reporter diff entry (i.e. the field appears in `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` and `outbound_fields.py` mapping is exercised by the test fixture).
findings.finding: Section 4 names adding `reporter` to `outbound_fields.py` and `dispatch_one.py::_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` (so the differ emits reporter) as explicit deliverables, but no AC verifies that the differ actually emits the reporter field or that `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` contains it.
findings.location: Section 4 ('reporter is added to outbound_fields.py + dispatch_one.py::_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST') vs Acceptance Criteria

findings.finding: The /user/search bootstrap (Section 3) has no acceptance criterion — its behavior is entirely unverified by the plan's ACs.
findings.location: Section 3 — 'transient /user/search bootstrap'; Acceptance Criteria section

findings.finding: Section 3 describes a best-effort transient `/user/search` bootstrap as a deliverable (with its own behavior: runs on email-present/no-jira-mapping, degrades on hidden email), but no acceptance criterion covers this path — neither the happy path (bootstrap resolves an accountId) nor the degradation path (hidden email → falls through to unassigned).
findings.location: Section 3 ('`/user/search` bootstrap') vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC4)

findings.finding: Section 4 explicitly calls out that reporter must be added to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` in `dispatch_one.py` or it will be stripped before the apply handler runs — making the allowlist entry a required deliverable. No AC verifies that reporter actually reaches the apply handler (i.e., is not stripped by the allowlist gate).
findings.location: Section 4 ('Reporter by accountId where permitted') vs Acceptance Criteria AC4; `file_impact` `dispatch_one.py`

findings.finding: The transient `/user/search` bootstrap (Section 3 of the plan) introduces new conditional logic — call `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` when an identity has email but no Jira mapping, degrade silently on hidden-email/no-result — but NO acceptance criterion covers this path at all (not even happy path). If this code is written and broken, every AC can still pass green.
findings.location: What › Section 3 (`/user/search` bootstrap); `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_rest.py`; Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs are silent on bootstrap)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] When a local assignee resolves to an identity with `email` but no `{provider:"jira"}` mapping, a stubbed `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` call (a) returning one result causes `validate_assignee_exists` to return that accountId (spy asserts one call); (b) returning empty or raising `HTTPError` causes `validate_assignee_exists` to return `None` and degrade to unassigned without raising (spy asserts one call, no exception propagated).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Section 4's `set_reporter` implementation to use `_direct_rest_put_raw` (not `_direct_rest_put`) for `PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{jira_key}` — `_direct_rest_put` wraps its body in `{"value": ...}` (valid only for issue-properties endpoints) and the module docstring itself forbids its use for the issue-update endpoint.
findings.finding: Section 4 specifies `acli_rest._direct_rest_put(f"/rest/api/3/issue/{jira_key}", {"fields": {"reporter": {"accountId": ...}}})` for the reporter sub-call, but `_direct_rest_put` wraps its body as `{"value": data}` (acli_rest.py:95), producing a malformed request body. The correct helper for a bare PUT to `/rest/api/3/issue/{key}` is `_direct_rest_put_raw`, already used by `set_parent` for the identical endpoint.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId), acli_rest.py line 95
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `acli_rest._direct_rest_put(f"/rest/api/3/issue/{jira_key}", {"fields": {"reporter": ...}})` with `self._direct_rest_put_raw(f"/rest/api/3/issue/{jira_key}", {"fields": {"reporter": {"accountId": account_id}}})`, mirroring the pattern already used by `set_parent` on acli.py:670.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the reporter 4xx catch and `HandlerResult(soft_failed=True)` return are emitted from a new phase inside `update_one` (requiring a return-type change) or from a new branch in `handle_update` in `apply_handlers.py`, and update the file_impact and section 4 to be consistent.
findings.finding: The plan places `HandlerResult(soft_failed=True)` for a reporter 4xx in the description of `update_one` (dispatch_one.py), but `update_one` currently returns `dict | None` — not a `HandlerResult`. `HandlerResult` is defined in `apply_handlers.py`. The file_impact simultaneously assigns 'reporter apply + dual-mode alert_store degradation' to `apply_handlers.py`. The plan does not resolve whether the return type of `update_one` must change, or whether the reporter isolation/catch lives in `handle_update` in `apply_handlers.py` — two architecturally different placements with different isolation properties.
findings.location: Section 4 / file_impact: apply_handlers.py vs dispatch_one.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a grep or explicit design note) that `REBAR_ROOT` is reliably set in every reconciler invocation context where `validate_assignee_exists` runs, OR specify that `repo_root` should be threaded into `AcliClient.__init__` from the reconciler's already-resolved repo root so the ambient fallback is not required.
findings.finding: The plan says `validate_assignee_exists` uses `rebar.config.repo_root()` as the AMBIENT reconcile repo with 'NO repo_root needs threading through AcliClient.__init__'. However, no grep of the reconciler package finds any existing call to `rebar.config.repo_root()` (as opposed to `rebar.config.load_config` or `from rebar.config import ...`), and `AcliClient` is instantiated in tests and the reconciler with no repo context. The 'ambient' claim relies on `REBAR_ROOT` env being set, but the reconciler subprocess environment is not verified to set it in all invocation paths (e.g., test stubs, CI runner). An unset `REBAR_ROOT` causes `rebar.config.repo_root()` to fall back to `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` of the CWD — which may be the reconciler's CWD, not the rebar store repo.
findings.location: Section 2 (Assignee by accountId) — 'using the AMBIENT reconcile repo, so NO repo_root needs threading through AcliClient.__init__'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify (or fix) the ambient `rebar.config.repo_root()` call in `validate_assignee_exists`: either demonstrate it equals `BatchApplyContext.repo_root` at runtime in all supported environments, or thread `repo_root` from `AcliClient` (set once at construction from the reconciler's resolved root) so the identity lookup targets the correct ticket store.
findings.finding: The plan instructs `validate_assignee_exists` to call `rebar.config.repo_root()` as the AMBIENT reconcile repo without justifying why it is guaranteed to equal `BatchApplyContext.repo_root` (the actual reconcile repo_root threaded through the call stack). In multi-repo or test-isolated scenarios these can diverge, causing the identity lookup to scan the wrong ticket store and silently miss a mapping.
findings.location: ## What / §2 'Assignee by accountId at the single Jira chokepoint'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `self._repo_root` in `AcliClient.__init__` from the already-resolved `repo_root` the reconciler passes when constructing the client (mirrors how `BatchApplyContext.repo_root` is set), then pass `repo_root=self._repo_root` to `identity.jira_account_id(...)` — no threading through every call site is needed since `__init__` is the single construction point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the 'ambient config.repo_root()' pattern inside `validate_assignee_exists` vs the established explicit repo_root threading used everywhere else in the reconciler, OR thread repo_root through AcliClient (e.g., as an optional constructor kwarg or a per-call param) to match the existing pattern and make the mapping lookup deterministic in tests.
findings.finding: The plan's §2 'ambient repo_root' pattern — calling `rebar.config.repo_root()` inside `validate_assignee_exists` with no parameter threading — is architecturally novel vs every other reconciler site, which explicitly passes repo_root through the call chain (BatchApplyContext.repo_root, create_one(repo_root=...), dispatch_one.py REBAR_ROOT fallback chain). The plan asserts this is safe because the 'AMBIENT reconcile repo' is the same repo, but provides no justification that REBAR_ROOT is guaranteed to be set at the validate_assignee_exists call site, nor acknowledges the divergence from the established explicit-threading pattern. In a test harness or an invocation without REBAR_ROOT, config.repo_root() would fall back to git-toplevel of CWD or CWD itself, silently resolving to the wrong repo and returning None from jira_account_id — causing the mapping fast-path to always miss without any error.
findings.location: §2 ('Assignee by accountId at the single Jira chokepoint') — 'using the AMBIENT reconcile repo, so NO repo_root needs threading through AcliClient.__init__ or the call sites'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `repo_root: Path | None = None` parameter to `AcliClient.__init__` (stored as `self._repo_root`), passed from the reconciler's `ctx.repo_root`. In `validate_assignee_exists`, call `identity.jira_account_id(assignee, repo_root=self._repo_root)`. This matches how every other repo-root-dependent operation in the reconciler is wired and removes the ambient-resolution risk.

findings.finding: The `jira_account_id` function has two distinct match branches — match by identity ticket-id and match by `email` (case-insensitive) — but AC1 tests them as a single assertion ('returns the identity's jira accountId for a matching local assignee'). The email-match branch is separately specified behavior that could silently regress; a fixture matching only by ticket-id would leave the email branch untested.
findings.location: What › Section 1 (`jira_account_id` spec); Acceptance Criteria › AC1
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'jira_account_id returns the identity's jira accountId for a matching local assignee and None otherwise (test asserts both)' with: 'jira_account_id returns the accountId (a) when local_assignee matches the identity ticket-id, (b) when local_assignee matches the identity email case-insensitively (e.g., "User@Example.COM" matches "user@example.com"), and (c) None when neither matches (test asserts all three cases).'

findings.finding: The best-effort `/user/search` bootstrap in Section 3 has an underspecified data-flow: when `jira_account_id` returns `None` (no mapping), the fallback inside `validate_assignee_exists` needs the identity's `email` to query `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>`. But `validate_assignee_exists` is called with only the raw `local_assignee` string (a ticket-id or email). If it is a ticket-id (the common case), a second identity lookup is needed to retrieve the email — but the plan does not specify where this second lookup happens or which function performs it. The flow 'an identity has an email but no `{provider:"jira"}` accountId mapping' requires resolving the identity object from the local_assignee string, which `jira_account_id` already does internally, but the plan places the `/user/search` call 'inside `validate_assignee_exists`'s fallback' where only the local_assignee string is available.
findings.location: Section 3 ('best-effort /user/search bootstrap'); AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction: explicitly state in the plan whether reporter's try/except + alert_store degradation lives in `dispatch_one.py::update_one` or `apply_handlers.py`, and remove the conflicting assignment from whichever file does NOT own it.
findings.finding: Section 4 prose says reporter degradation/soft-fail lives inside `update_one` in `dispatch_one.py` ('a separate dispatch branch … update_one routes it to set_reporter'), but `file_impact` separately assigns 'reporter apply + dual-mode alert_store degradation' to `apply_handlers.py`. AC4 says 'the handler records an alert_store entry', pointing at apply_handlers.py. These three locations give contradictory homes for the same logic, which would send an implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Section 4 (reporter sub-call prose) vs file_impact entry for `apply_handlers.py` vs AC4
findings.suggested_fix: If the pattern mirrors existing label/comment sub-call handlers (which live in apply_handlers.py), move the dispatch reference to apply_handlers.py and remove 'reporter apply + dual-mode alert_store degradation' from dispatch_one.py's file_impact entry; update Section 4 prose to say 'apply_handlers.py routes reporter to set_reporter and catches 4xx'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the reporter try/except + alert_store.append lives inside update_one (dispatch_one.py, where it has no access to ctx.repo_root/pass_id) or as a post-update_one sub-call in handle_update (apply_handlers.py, where ctx is available), and ensure the chosen location has access to both the HTTP error AND the BatchApplyContext fields needed to record the alert.
findings.finding: apply_handlers.py is listed in file_impact for 'reporter apply + dual-mode alert_store degradation', but the plan also says reporter is routed via a dedicated sub-call in update_one (dispatch_one.py). The AC specifies the catch of HTTP 4xx must NOT affect other fields 'in the same mutation' — but update_one in dispatch_one.py is called as a monolith from handle_update in apply_handlers.py, which already has its own except clauses. The plan does not specify WHERE exactly the try/except for the reporter sub-call lives (inside update_one in dispatch_one.py, or as a post-update_one call in handle_update in apply_handlers.py), creating ambiguity about the isolation boundary.
findings.location: Section 4 (reporter isolation); file_impact apply_handlers.py; AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend placing the set_reporter call as a dedicated step in handle_update (apply_handlers.py) AFTER the update_one call succeeds, mirroring the labels/comments pattern. This gives access to ctx.repo_root and ctx.pass_id for the alert_store.append, and keeps the isolation clean: reporter failure cannot abort the scalar update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to §4 that the reporter handler must supply `repo_root=ctx.repo_root` (from `BatchApplyContext`) to `alert_store.append(...)`, consistent with the existing `AssigneeNotFoundError` handler at apply_handlers.py line 185.
findings.finding: The plan's reporter degradation path calls `alert_store.append(...)` with kind/fields but does not address that `alert_store.append` requires `repo_root: Path` as a required positional argument (not an optional kwarg). The plan does not specify how `repo_root` is supplied in the reporter handler context inside `apply_handlers.py`.
findings.location: ## What / §4 'Reporter by accountId — a DEDICATED REST sub-call'; ## Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the `apply_handlers.py` reporter sub-call catch block, use `_load_alert_store().append({"kind": "outbound-reporter-not-permitted", ...}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)` — identical pattern to the existing AssigneeNotFoundError handler.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `repo_root` (required by `alert_store.append`) is available at the reporter 4xx catch site — either by adding `repo_root` to `update_one`'s signature, placing the catch in `handle_update` where `ctx.repo_root` is accessible, or describing an alternative mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the reporter failure alert is recorded via `alert_store.append(..., kind='outbound-reporter-not-permitted', fields jira_key, field='reporter', reason)` but omits `repo_root` — which the existing `alert_store.append(record, repo_root=Path)` API requires. At the call site (inside the reporter sub-call, which is inside `update_one` or a phase thereof), `repo_root` is not available on the call stack unless threaded through. The plan explicitly says 'NO `repo_root` needs threading through `AcliClient.__init__`' only for the assignee path, leaving reporter silent on this.
findings.location: Section 4 / file_impact: apply_handlers.py (reporter apply + dual-mode alert_store degradation)
findings.suggested_fix: Place the reporter 4xx catch in `handle_update` (apply_handlers.py) where `ctx.repo_root` is already available, matching the existing `AssigneeNotFoundError` soft-fail pattern on lines 173-189.

findings.finding: The reporter apply injection point in apply_handlers.py is architecturally underspecified: §4 says 'the apply handler must catch BOTH failure modes (HTTP 4xx OR subprocess.CalledProcessError)' but the existing pipeline routes all field mutations through update_one → _update_one_scalar_update → client.update_issue → _run_acli, where CalledProcessError is already caught internally. The plan does not specify whether reporter gets a dedicated sub-phase wrapping update_one, a new call before/after it, or a try/except inside handle_update — leaving placement ambiguous.
findings.location: Section 4 — 'Reporter by accountId where permitted'; apply_handlers.py:handle_update

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the tie-breaking and ambiguity contract for `jira_account_id`: e.g. ticket-id match takes priority over email match; if both criteria independently match different identities, return `None` (ambiguous) and log a warning — mirroring `_match_by_email`'s ambiguity→None pattern.
findings.finding: `jira_account_id` is specified to match 'an identity whose ticket id equals `local_assignee` OR whose `email` equals it (case-insensitive)' but the plan does not specify precedence or de-duplication when both criteria match different identities (ticket-id match on identity A, email match on identity B). This is a plausible real-world case (a user whose email happens to match another identity's id) and would silently return one arbitrarily.
findings.location: ## What / §1 'Provider-neutral resolution seam'; ## Acceptance Criteria AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and state definitively whether ACLI Go's `jira workitem edit` exposes a `--reporter` flag (via `jira workitem edit --help`, ACLI changelog, or a live probe), and cite that verification in the plan — if it does, evaluate whether the simpler scalar-field approach suffices; if it doesn't, change 'may not expose' to 'does not expose (verified: <source>)'.
findings.finding: The §4 isolated-REST-sub-call architecture for reporter is premised on the unverified assertion that 'ACLI's workitem edit may not expose --reporter'. This 'may not expose' is the load-bearing capability claim that justifies the entire isolated-sub-call design (separate REST PUT, distinct dispatch branch, isolated 403 catch). If ACLI does expose --reporter via `jira workitem edit --reporter <accountId>`, the simpler approach of adding reporter to the existing scalar update_issue call (with a 403-at-ACLI-exit-code soft-fail) would work, and the isolated sub-call is over-engineering. The plan does not cite a verification source (ACLI changelog, live probe, --help output) for this assertion.
findings.location: §4 ('Reporter by accountId — a DEDICATED REST sub-call') — 'ACLI's workitem edit may not expose --reporter'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the reporter sub-call error boundary to cover 5xx and network-level errors (`urllib.error.URLError`, socket timeout) — not just 4xx `HTTPError` — so the isolation guarantee holds for all failure modes, and add a corresponding test scenario.
findings.finding: The `set_reporter` REST sub-call specifies catching `urllib.error.HTTPError` (4xx) only. HTTP 5xx responses and low-level network/connection errors (e.g., `urllib.error.URLError`, socket timeout) are not addressed and would propagate as unhandled exceptions, potentially hard-failing the sync and affecting unrelated fields despite the plan's stated isolation guarantee.
findings.location: What §4 Reporter by accountId — failure mode paragraph; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `set_reporter` call in `except (urllib.error.HTTPError, urllib.error.URLError) as exc` (or a broader `except OSError`), log the error, append an alert entry (e.g., kind `outbound-reporter-error`, with `reason=str(exc)`), and return `HandlerResult(soft_failed=True)` — mirroring the 4xx path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `from rebar import config` (or `from rebar._commands import identity`) inside `validate_assignee_exists` does not create a circular import in the reconciler package context, and add a lazy-import note to the plan (mirroring the pattern in `outbound_differ.py:559` or `_concurrency.py:54`).
findings.finding: The plan says validate_assignee_exists will call `identity.jira_account_id(assignee, repo_root=rebar.config.repo_root())` using the 'AMBIENT reconcile repo, so NO repo_root needs threading through AcliClient.__init__' — but acli.py currently has NO import of rebar.config, making this a new cross-package import that is unacknowledged and could introduce a subtle circular-import risk (rebar.config → rebar._commands.identity → rebar._commands._seam → ...) in the reconciler context.
findings.location: Section 2 (validate_assignee_exists); src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to Section 2 specifying that the import of `identity` must be lazy (inside the method body, not at module level), e.g.: `from rebar._commands import identity as _identity_mod; acct_id = _identity_mod.jira_account_id(...)`. This matches the pattern already used in other reconciler files that cross into the rebar package.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State 'Browse Users and Groups' global permission as an explicit precondition for Section 3's `/rest/api/3/user/search` bootstrap, verify the service account has it (or document the fallback behavior when it does not), and distinguish the 403-permission-denied case from a genuine no-result in the degradation path.
findings.finding: Section 3's `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` bootstrap is a NEW endpoint surface not present anywhere in the codebase. In Jira Cloud, this endpoint requires the **Browse Users and Groups** global permission, which is distinct from the 'Assign Issues' project permission the service account is already expected to hold. The plan does not state this permission as a precondition, and does not specify whether the team has verified the service account has it — leaving feasibility unverified for this specific surface.
findings.location: Section 3 (transient /user/search bootstrap), acli_rest.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly rank §3 as either (a) required — add a covering AC — or (b) optional/stretch — move it to an explicit 'nice-to-have' sub-list and remove acli_rest.py's bootstrap changes from file_impact if not required.
findings.finding: Section 3 is included in the 'What' list and in file_impact (acli_rest.py) but carries no AC and is labeled 'best-effort, TRANSIENT' — leaving the executor without a clear priority signal: is implementing /user/search bootstrap required to close this ticket, or is it optional?
findings.location: What §3; Scope 'In:'; file_impact acli_rest.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC that exercises the accountId fast-path and set_reporter against a live (or recorded-cassette) Jira instance, OR add a named child ticket with a rationale sentence explaining why live validation is deferred (e.g. 'live Jira integration verified in ticket REBAR-NNN once a test-project accountId is available').
findings.finding: Every AC exercises the new `validate_assignee_exists` identity-mapping fast-path and the reporter `set_reporter` REST sub-call exclusively via stubs/mocks ('stubbed Jira accountId', 'stubbed reporter PUT'). The plan explicitly states 'Tests use a fixture identity + a stubbed client (no live Jira)' with no named child ticket or formal deferral rationale. Per E6(f), for a cutover that defaults to a new code path with a live external boundary (Jira REST API), offline-only stubs do not prove the changed path executes correctly in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs); Scope last sentence; What §2, §4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Live validation deferred to REBAR-NNN (Jira integration smoke test). Acceptance for this ticket is offline-stub coverage only; a follow-up ticket will execute validate_assignee_exists and set_reporter against the test-project Jira instance and assert the returned accountId matches the fixture identity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path for the `validate_assignee_exists` behavioral change — e.g., a config flag to disable the identity fast-path, an expand-contract sequence (add fast-path → observe → remove old path), or a documented code-rollback procedure — so the behavior is reversible without a full redeploy.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `validate_assignee_exists` to skip assignable-search when an identity mapping resolves (a behavioral change for all callers), but provides no rollback/back-out path if the new fast-path causes regressions in production — acknowledgement alone is present but T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: What §2 / `acli.py` / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single config key (e.g., `identity_mapping_enabled: bool = True` in rebar config) that gates the fast-path inside `validate_assignee_exists`; document its default and how to disable it. This provides a rollback lever without a code change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Formally justify and mitigate the new `acli.py` → `identity.py` cross-component dependency (e.g., define an `IdentityResolver` protocol/interface so `AcliClient` depends on an abstraction, not a concrete import), AND capture the provider-neutral mapping-seam architectural decision in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-component dependency (reconciler `acli.py` importing `identity.py` from `_commands`) and an ambient `rebar.config.repo_root()` coupling inside `AcliClient` — both are described in passing but neither is formally justified, mitigated via an interface boundary, nor captured in an ADR or design-doc update, as required for a novel architectural pattern.
findings.location: What §2 / `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py` + `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli.py` / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small `IdentityResolver` protocol in the reconciler package (e.g., `Callable[[str], str | None]`), inject it into `AcliClient.__init__` defaulting to `identity.jira_account_id`, and add a one-paragraph ADR entry noting why ambient `repo_root()` was chosen over explicit threading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum a `logger.warning(...)` (or equivalent structured log) inside `resolve_mapping` and `jira_account_id` when an exception is caught and swallowed, so operators can distinguish 'no mapping found' from 'identity store error'.
findings.finding: Both `resolve_mapping` and `jira_account_id` are specified to 'Never raise' — meaning all internal errors (malformed identity file, missing `mappings` key, unexpected schema) are silently swallowed and the caller receives `None`, with no log line, metric, or alert. Operators have no signal that identity resolution is failing rather than simply absent.
findings.location: What §1 Provider-neutral resolution seam; `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a socket/request timeout (e.g., `timeout=5s`) for the `/user/search` bootstrap call in `acli_rest.py`, and treat a timeout as a non-fatal miss (degrade to existing path) — matching the stated best-effort semantics.
findings.finding: The transient `/user/search` bootstrap REST call inside `validate_assignee_exists`'s fallback path has no specified timeout, retry, or rate-limit handling. A slow or hung Jira API response will block the sync thread for an unbounded duration.
findings.location: What §3 `/user/search` bootstrap; `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_rest.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Pass `timeout=5` (seconds) to the `urllib.request.urlopen` call (or equivalent) and catch `socket.timeout` / `urllib.error.URLError` as a no-result case, logging a warning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal second-provider assertion to AC1 (e.g., a fixture identity with `{provider:'github', external_id:'gh-42'}` and assert `resolve_mapping('github','gh-42')` returns that identity's id) so the provider-neutral claim is end-state-observable within this plan's own tests.
findings.finding: The plan claims `resolve_mapping` is 'provider-neutral' (works for any provider, not just Jira), but no AC exercises it with a non-Jira provider. The only AC for the seam (AC1) validates Jira-keyed lookups. If the implementation hard-codes Jira-specific logic inside what is described as a generic seam, the provider-neutral claim is unverifiable within this plan's own tests.
findings.location: What §1 (`resolve_mapping`) and AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'resolve_mapping returns the identity for any provider key — additionally assert with a fixture identity whose mapping uses provider="github" (or any non-Jira value) to confirm no Jira-specific guard exists in the implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the tracker-scan loop (`os.listdir` + `_reduce` per entry + field predicate) in `identity.py` into a shared `_find_identity(predicate, tracker)` helper, then implement `jira_account_id` by composing it rather than writing a third standalone scan.
findings.finding: `jira_account_id` in `identity.py` would introduce a third copy of the tracker-scan loop (os.listdir + `_reduce` per entry + field checks) that already exists in the same file as `_match_by_email` (line 119) and is used again inline in `resolve_current_identity` (line 316). Adding a third standalone scan is NIH duplication — the scan pattern should be extracted into a shared `_find_identity(predicate)` helper, or `jira_account_id` should compose directly from existing helpers.
findings.location: Section 1 (`jira_account_id` in `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `def _find_identity(predicate, tracker: str) -> str | None` that iterates, reduces, and applies `predicate(state) -> bool`, returning the sole match or `None` on 0/2+. `_match_by_email` and `jira_account_id` both become one-liners on top of it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make `resolve_mapping` private (`_resolve_mapping`) or inline it into `jira_account_id` until a second confirmed consumer (2f13 in scope) materializes, to satisfy the Rule-of-Three before promoting it as a named public seam.
findings.finding: `resolve_mapping(provider, external_id)` is introduced as a 'provider-neutral' abstraction, but the plan confirms only ONE current consumer in this done-definition: `jira_account_id` calls it internally. The only other named consumer ('also consumed inbound by 2f13') is a future ticket explicitly out of scope here. With a single confirmed call-site, the Rule-of-Three test for a standalone public abstraction fails — it is premature.
findings.location: Section 1 (`resolve_mapping` in `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`); AC1

findings.finding: The plan asserts the reporter `subprocess.CalledProcessError` failure must be caught in `apply_handlers.py` and recorded as `outbound-reporter-not-permitted`. However, `reporter` will be passed as one kwarg among others in a single `client.update_issue(**fields)` call (via `_call_with_retry` in `dispatch_one.py`). A `CalledProcessError` from ACLI rejecting `--reporter` is not distinguishable from other field failures at the `handle_update` boundary, and the current handler only catches `urllib.error.HTTPError` and `AssigneeNotFoundError`. The plan does not specify where in the call stack (`acli.py:update_issue`, `dispatch_one.py:_update_one_scalar_update`, or `apply_handlers.py:handle_update`) the `CalledProcessError` catch is inserted, nor how to isolate a reporter-specific failure from other fields failing in the same call.
findings.location: Plan §What/4 — 'the apply handler (`apply_handlers.py`) must catch BOTH failure modes'
findings.suggested_fix: Route reporter as a separate, isolated REST or ACLI call (not mixed into the shared `workitem edit` kwarg dict) so its failure can be caught and recorded locally without affecting other field updates. Alternatively, add an explicit `try/except subprocess.CalledProcessError` around the reporter-including `update_issue` path and retry without the reporter field.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `reporter` is dispatched as a bundled field inside `client.update_issue()` or as a separate sub-call (like labels/comments in `update_one`). If bundled, a `subprocess.CalledProcessError` from ACLI `workitem edit` would fail the entire mutation in `_update_one_scalar_update`, and the proposed catch in `apply_handlers.handle_update` would suppress ALL field failures — not only reporter — producing an under-specified and potentially too-broad exception swallow. If it is a separate sub-call (the model needed to achieve the stated isolation), the plan should say so explicitly, since `_update_one_filter_fields` + `_update_one_scalar_update` today have no reporter-specific dispatch branch.
findings.location: Section 4 ('Reporter by accountId where permitted') and file_impact: apply_handlers.py, dispatch_one.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Reporter is dispatched as a dedicated sub-call AFTER the scalar update (parallel to label/comment dispatch in `update_one`), not bundled in the main `client.update_issue` invocation, so its CalledProcessError / HTTP-4xx catch is scoped to reporter only and does not suppress other field failures.'

findings.finding: The reporter 403/400 interception mechanism is broken as specified. The plan routes `reporter` through the standard `update_issue(**fields)` call (via the allowlist), then says a 403/400 on the reporter field is swallowed. But `client.update_issue` issues a single REST call for all fields together — Jira returns a top-level 403/400 that aborts the entire mutation, not a field-specific reporter error. A 403 on Modify-Reporter will therefore cause the whole ticket update to fail (not just the reporter field). Reporter needs its own dedicated REST call (mirroring how `parent` is popped before the allowlist and routed through `client.set_parent`), so that its 403 can be caught and soft-failed independently without aborting the scalar field update.
findings.location: Section 4 (Reporter by accountId) / `apply_handlers.py`, `dispatch_one.py` `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST`

findings.finding: The plan places the `/user/search` bootstrap implementation 'in `acli_rest.py`', but reading `acli_rest.py` confirms it contains only the `AcliRestMixin` REST transport primitives (`_direct_rest_get`, `_direct_rest_put`, etc.) — no application logic. The entry point for `validate_assignee_exists` (where the bootstrap would actually execute) is in `acli.py`, not `acli_rest.py`. Placing the implementation in `acli_rest.py` would introduce application logic into a pure transport mixin, breaking the module's stated contract.
findings.location: Plan §What/3 — 'a best-effort `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` lookup (in `acli_rest.py`, used inside `validate_assignee_exists`'s fallback)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
